title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Ancient Japanese rice fermentation
description: Niigata coast — kuchikamizake (chewed-rice sake) archaeological evidence from Yayoi period ~300 BCE.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Ancient Japanese rice fermentation
description: Kyoto — imperial court sake brewing; Engi-shiki (927 CE) documents official sake production system.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Medieval temple + commercial brewing
description: Osaka / Nara — Buddhist temple breweries perfect the Bodai-moto method 12th–15th century.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Medieval temple + commercial brewing
description: Nada (Kobe region, Hyogo) — becomes Japan's largest sake-producing region by Edo period thanks to Miyamizu hard water.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Edo-period Taru Kaisen shipping
description: Edo (Tokyo) — Taru Kaisen wooden sake-barrel ships run the Nada-to-Edo route from 1619 onward.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Edo-period Taru Kaisen shipping
description: Nada (Hyogo) — departure port. Osaka Bay winds + currents enable 5-day voyage.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Post-WWII global spread
description: San Francisco — 19th-century Japanese immigration brings sake to the US West Coast. Modern craft breweries run by non-Japanese brewers now.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Post-WWII global spread
description: New York — premium-sake import market boom 2010s.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Post-WWII global spread
description: London — UK sake market grew 200%+ in the last decade.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: Japanese names
description: Japanese — 'sake' (酒) is the general Japanese word for alcohol; 'nihonshu' (日本酒) = 'Japanese alcohol' is the specific term.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: Japanese names
description: Niigata dialect — regional sake-style names like 'tanrei-karakuchi' (light-dry).

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: Japanese names
description: English — 'sake' (borrowed unchanged). 'Saké' sometimes written with accent but rare in Japanese rōmaji.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Niigata — #1 sake-producing prefecture by volume + premium reputation.
